regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III (clinically positive nodes)

trial_data.efficacy: 12-month EFS: 83.7% vs. 57.2% with adjuvant nivolumab (P < .01). MPR rate: 59%. OS data not mature.
reference_numbers: NEOSYS 1-7 (Blank CU, N Engl J Med 2024;391:1696-1708)

regimens.notes: This regimen is category 1 only for initial presentation of stage III disease with clinically positive nodes that have not received prior targeted or immune therapy [NEOSYS-1]. Ipilimumab 1 mg/kg + nivolumab 3 mg/kg is generally preferred over ipilimumab 3 mg/kg + nivolumab 1 mg/kg, as it is associated with similar pathologic response and RFS rates and lower toxicities [NEOSYS-1]. MPR following 2 doses is associated with >90% 3-year RFS with no additional adjuvant therapy; optimal adjuvant therapy is not clear but can include anti-PD-1 monotherapy or observation for MPR, or anti-PD-1 or dabrafenib/trametinib for those lacking MPR [NEOSYS-1]. For low-volume in-transit disease, the high risk of toxicities associated with combination regimens may outweigh the benefit [NEOSYS-1].

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III

trial_data.efficacy: 2-year EFS: 72% vs. 49% with adjuvant-only pembrolizumab (P < .01). MPR rate: 53%. OS data not mature.
reference_numbers: NEOSYS 5 (Patel SP, N Engl J Med 2023;388:813-823)

regimens.notes: It is unclear which neoadjuvant regimen is more active since they have not been directly compared [NEOSYS-1]. The SWOG S1801 trial assigned patients to complete a total of one year of systemic pembrolizumab irrespective of pathologic response status; withholding adjuvant therapy following MPR was NOT tested in this trial and is not routinely recommended [NEOSYS-6]. A 21% pCR rate was reported after 3 doses of neoadjuvant pembrolizumab [NEOSYS-4].

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III

trial_data.efficacy: Activity demonstrated but not studied in larger randomized studies with modern comparators.

regimens.notes: Studies have demonstrated efficacy for nivolumab neoadjuvant monotherapy (1–3 doses every 2 weeks); however, these have not been studied in larger randomized studies, with modern comparators, or against active adjuvant therapy [NEOSYS-5].

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III

trial_data.efficacy: Activity demonstrated; not studied in larger randomized studies with modern comparators.

regimens.notes: Studies have demonstrated efficacy for nivolumab and relatlimab (2 doses every 4 weeks); however, these have not been studied in larger randomized studies, with modern comparators, or against active adjuvant therapy [NEOSYS-5]. In one small study, adjuvant nivolumab/relatlimab-rmbw was given for a total of 1 year of therapy. Adjustment based on pathologic response status has not been studied [NEOSYS-6].

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III

trial_data.efficacy: Active in neoadjuvant setting for 4–12 weeks of preoperative therapy.
reference_numbers: NEOSYS 5 (Amaria RN, Lancet Oncol 2018;19:181-193; Long GV, Lancet Oncol 2019;20:961-971)

regimens.notes: If immunotherapy is contraindicated, dabrafenib and trametinib could be considered for a short course (4–12 weeks) of preoperative therapy; however, this approach has not been studied in comparison with adjuvant dabrafenib and trametinib [NEOSYS-1, NEOSYS-5]. Other BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations can be considered in the event of unacceptable toxicities to dabrafenib/trametinib or based on side effect profiles [NEOSYS-1].

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III/IV resectable

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then nivolumab maintenance

regimens.guideline_category: Useful in Certain Circumstances (for oligometastatic resected stage IV)

trial_data.efficacy: Ipilimumab 3 mg/kg + nivolumab 1 mg/kg improved RFS compared with nivolumab or ipilimumab alone for resected stage IV melanoma.

regimens.notes: Ipilimumab + nivolumab combination did not improve EFS compared to nivolumab in resected stage III/IV melanoma with one possible exception: in the IMMUNED randomized phase II study of adjuvant therapy for resected stage IV melanoma, ipilimumab 3 mg/kg and nivolumab 1 mg/kg improved RFS compared with nivolumab or ipilimumab alone [ADJSYS 2]. For patients who have previously received systemic therapy for cutaneous melanoma, selection of adjuvant treatment regimen should be informed by response to prior systemic therapies [ADJSYS-1].

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III/IV resectable

trial_data.efficacy: 1-year RFS: 71% vs. 61% (nivolumab vs. ipilimumab); HR = 0.65 (95% CI: 0.51–0.83; P < .001). 1-year DMFS: 80% vs. 73%; HR = 0.73 (95% CI: 0.55–0.95). Grade 3–4 AEs: 25% vs. 55%.

regimens.notes: Category 1 for patients with AJCC 7th Edition stage IIIB/C disease. Category 2A for other stage III subgroups and satellite/in-transit disease. Nivolumab has shown a clinically significant improvement in RFS compared to high-dose ipilimumab [ADJSYS-1]. CheckMate 238 included 155 patients with in-transit melanoma only; nivolumab showed improvement in RFS compared to high-dose ipilimumab. The NCCN Panel agrees it is appropriate to extend the indication for adjuvant anti-PD-1 therapy to patients with clinical or macroscopic satellite/in-transit disease [ADJSYS-1]. Duration: 12 months.

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III/IV resectable
regimens.notes: Category 1 for patients with clinically detected nodal metastases. For patients with clinically occult nodal disease, category 1 is limited to stage IIIA with at least one nodal metastasis >1 mm or stage IIIB/C per AJCC 7th Edition staging. Patients with in-transit metastases were excluded, so category 2A in this setting [ADJSYS-1]. Pembrolizumab has shown a clinically significant improvement in RFS compared to placebo, but its impact on OS has not yet been reported [ADJSYS-1]. Duration: 12 months.

regimens.regimen_name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (Adjuvant — Preferred for BRAF V600 Mutant)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III resectable
regimens.notes: Category 1 for stage III clinically positive nodes. For occult nodal disease, category 1 limited to stage IIIA with ≥1 mm nodal metastasis or stage IIIB/C per AJCC 7th Edition. If BRAF V600 mutation positive, other BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations can be considered in the event of unacceptable toxicities to dabrafenib/trametinib or based on side effect profiles [ADJSYS-1]. BRAF V600K mutated metastatic melanoma may have slightly lower response/benefit than V600E. Duration: 12 months.

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III/IV resectable with prior anti-PD-1 exposure

drugs.name: Ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then every 12 weeks for up to 3 years

regimens.notes: Ipilimumab is recommended only if the patient has prior exposure to anti-PD-1 therapy. The recommended dose is 3 mg/kg (extrapolated from ECOG 1609 preliminary data suggesting similar efficacy with lower toxicity) [ADJSYS-1]. In EORTC 18071 using 10 mg/kg, grade 3–4 AEs were 54% and fatal AEs occurred in 1% of patients. In situations where adjuvant ipilimumab may be an option (eg, patients who progress during anti-PD-1 therapy with resectable disease), the recommended dose is 3 mg/kg [ADJSYS-1].

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then 240 mg Q2W or 480 mg Q4W maintenance

trial_data.efficacy: 4-year OS: 53% (nivo/ipi) vs. 46% (nivo) vs. 30% (ipi). ORR: 58% vs. 45% vs. 19%. Median PFS: 11.5 vs. 6.9 vs. 2.9 months. Grade 3–5 AEs: 59% vs. 22% vs. 28%. Grade 3–5 irAEs: 54% vs. 18%.

regimens.notes: Preferred first-line option for unresectable or metastatic melanoma. Nivolumab 1 mg/kg + ipilimumab 3 mg/kg has demonstrated clinically meaningful intracranial activity [MELSYS-3]. Considerations include higher efficacy but higher toxicity compared to anti-PD-1 monotherapy. Treatment duration: until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity [MELSYS-1]. Combination immune checkpoint blockade is associated with improved response rate, PFS, and OS compared with anti-PD-1 monotherapy [MELSYS-3].

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic

trial_data.efficacy: Improved PFS vs. nivolumab alone. Median PFS: 10.1 vs. 4.6 months; HR = 0.75 (95% CI: 0.62–0.92).

regimens.notes: First-line preferred option. Nivolumab and relatlimab-rmbw showed a 9%–12% ORR in patients with PD-1/PD-L1 refractory disease, supporting its role in later lines as well [MELSYS-3]. Treatment continues until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity.

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic

trial_data.efficacy: CheckMate 066: ORR 43% vs. 14% (DTIC); median PFS 5.1 vs. 2.2 months (P < .001); median OS 37.5 vs. 11.2 months (P < .001). CheckMate 067: 4-year OS 46%. Long-term survival up to ~40%.

regimens.notes: Both nivolumab 240 mg Q2W and 480 mg Q4W are FDA-approved flat doses. Treatment duration: until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. Responses are very durable, with many persisting after discontinuation. Anti-PD-1 therapy should be effective regardless of BRAF mutation status [MELSYS-3]. Subgroup analyses in CheckMate 067 showed similar efficacy regardless of PD-L1 expression, but descriptive analyses suggest patients with low PD-L1 may benefit more from combination therapy [MELSYS-4].

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic

trial_data.efficacy: ORR: 37% vs. 13% (P < .001). 2-year PFS: 31% vs. 14% (P < .0001). 2-year OS: 55% vs. 43% (P = .0008). Grade 3–5 treatment-related AEs: 17% vs. 20%.

regimens.notes: Results of KEYNOTE-006 support pembrolizumab as first-line therapy. Long-term follow-up showed that pembrolizumab provides long-term improvement in PFS and OS compared with ipilimumab monotherapy [MELSYS-1]. Median time to response approximately 3 months. Late responses can be observed more than a year after start of treatment. Most patients who achieve CR/PR and discontinue after 2 years maintain response with 2 years of follow-up [MELSYS-1].

regimens.regimen_name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (First-Line Metastatic/Unresectable — Other Recommended for BRAF V600 Mutant)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, BRAF V600 mutant

trial_data.n_patients: COMBI-d: 423 (211 dab/tram, 212 dab); COMBI-v: 704 (352 dab/tram, 352 vem)
trial_data.efficacy: COMBI-d: ORR 69% vs. 53% (P = .0014); median PFS 11.0 vs. 8.8 months (P = .0004); median OS 25.1 vs. 18.7 months (P = .0107). COMBI-v: ORR 64% vs. 51% (P < .001); median PFS 11.4 vs. 7.3 months (P < .001); OS: HR = 0.66 (P = .005). Grade 3–4 AEs: 48–52%.

regimens.notes: BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination therapy has been shown to have superior response rate, PFS, and OS compared with BRAF inhibitor monotherapy, as well as a similar or better toxicity profile. The DREAMseq trial (ECOG-ACRIN EA6134) demonstrated that combination dabrafenib/trametinib versus combination nivolumab/ipilimumab showed superior outcomes with immunotherapy first-line [MELSYS-4]. BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination therapy is considered appropriate for patients whose tumor harbors a BRAF mutation and who would benefit from a more rapid response [MELSYS-4].

regimens.regimen_name: Encorafenib/Binimetinib (First-Line Metastatic/Unresectable — Other Recommended for BRAF V600 Mutant)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, BRAF V600 mutant

trial_data.efficacy: Encorafenib/binimetinib vs. vemurafenib: ORR 64% vs. 41%; median PFS 14.9 vs. 7.3 months (P < .0001); median OS 33.6 vs. 16.9 months (P < .0001). Grade 3–4 AEs: 64%.

regimens.notes: In the COLUMBUS trial, encorafenib/binimetinib improved PFS and OS compared with vemurafenib monotherapy. Encorafenib/binimetinib vs. encorafenib monotherapy did not reach statistical significance for PFS (P = .050) or OS (P = .12) [MELSYS 1]. There are few data to inform selection among the BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination therapy options as none have been directly compared [MELSYS-4].

regimens.regimen_name: Vemurafenib/Cobimetinib (First-Line Metastatic/Unresectable — Other Recommended for BRAF V600 Mutant)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, BRAF V600 mutant

drugs.name: Cobimetinib
drugs.schedule: Once daily for 21 days on/7 days off

trial_data.efficacy: ORR: 70% vs. 50% (P < .0001); median PFS: 12.3 vs. 7.2 months (P < .0001); median OS: 22.3 vs. 17.4 months (P = .005). Grade 3–4 AEs: 75%.

regimens.notes: The combination showed improved PFS and OS compared to vemurafenib monotherapy. Diarrhea was a prominent toxicity in the combination arm, particularly during the first cycle [MELSYS-1]. There are few data to inform selection among the available BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations [MELSYS-4].

regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab (Second-Line/Subsequent Metastatic/Unresectable — Useful in Certain Circumstances)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: CA184-002: ORR 11% (ipi) vs. 2% (gp100); median OS 10.1 vs. 6.4 months (P < .001). CA184-169: 3 mg/kg ORR 12%, median OS 11.5 months; 10 mg/kg ORR 15%, median OS 15.7 months (P = .04).

regimens.notes: Although CheckMate 067 showed ipilimumab to have inferior outcomes compared to nivolumab/ipilimumab and nivolumab monotherapy in previously untreated patients, it is unclear whether the same would be true in patients who have progressed on prior systemic therapy, particularly anti-PD-1 therapy. For this reason, ipilimumab is included among acceptable options for previously treated patients [MELSYS-2]. Extended follow-up showed ipilimumab results in long-term survival in approximately 20% of patients [MELSYS-1].

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: Combination produced approximately 28% ORR vs. 8% for ipilimumab alone. 6-month PFS: 35% vs. 13%.

regimens.notes: A 94-patient trial randomized patients to ipilimumab and nivolumab versus ipilimumab alone following progression on anti-PD-1 therapy. The combination was associated with higher response rates and PFS [MELSYS-3]. This regimen is particularly useful for patients who progressed on prior anti-PD-1 monotherapy.

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

drugs.name: Lifileucel
drugs.schedule: One-time infusion following lymphodepleting chemotherapy and high-dose IL-2

trial_data.efficacy: Durable response rates in anti-PD-1 refractory melanoma. For patients with good performance status previously treated with anti-PD-1-based therapy and BRAF/MEK inhibition, TIL therapy should be considered.

regimens.notes: For patients with good performance status who have been previously treated with anti-PD-1-based therapy and BRAF/MEK inhibition (if BRAF V600 mutation present), TIL therapy (with high-dose IL-2) should be considered, based on durable response rates in anti-PD-1 refractory melanoma [MELSYS-3]. TIL therapy currently requires a resectable metastasis for TIL harvesting and includes the use of nonmyeloablative chemotherapy and high-dose IL-2. If cellular therapy will be part of treatment planning, early referral to a center with melanoma expertise should be strongly considered following anti-PD-1 progression [MELSYS-3].

regimens.regimen_name: Low-Dose Ipilimumab + Pembrolizumab (Second-Line/Subsequent — Preferred for Progression Following Anti-PD-1)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated with anti-PD-1

trial_data.efficacy: Long-term follow-up shows manageable safety and durable responses. Pembrolizumab plus ipilimumab following anti-PD-1/L1 failure produced approximately 25%–33% response rates, often durable.

regimens.notes: Recommended for patients with disease progression following anti-PD-1 therapy. The combination of ipilimumab/anti-PD-1 produces approximately 25%–33% response rates, often durable [MELSYS-3].

regimens.regimen_name: Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: Activity in patients with confirmed progression on a PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor given as monotherapy or in combination.

regimens.notes: Listed as an option for patients who have progressed on anti-PD-1-based therapy and BRAF/MEK inhibitors. This combination is listed as useful in certain circumstances for second-line or subsequent therapy [MELSYS-2].

regimens.regimen_name: T-VEC + Ipilimumab (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances for Injectable Metastases)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: ORR: 39% vs. 18% (P = .002). Median PFS: 8.2 vs. 6.4 months (P = .35). OS: not significantly different.

regimens.notes: Category 2B recommendation. Because results did not demonstrate improved PFS or OS, this is listed only as an option for second or subsequent-line therapy and is not a preferred option. The NCCN Panel voted not to include combination therapy with T-VEC plus systemic anti-PD-1 therapy as a recommended option due to insufficient randomized trial data on this specific combination [MELSYS-3].

regimens.regimen_name: Imatinib (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances for KIT Mutant)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, KIT mutation positive

trial_data.efficacy: 17%–30% ORR and 35%–57% disease control rate in patients with KIT-mutated or KIT-amplified metastatic melanomas. Most responses were of limited duration.

regimens.notes: Activating KIT mutations are rare in cutaneous melanoma but more common in mucosal and acral subtypes. Imatinib is listed as useful in certain circumstances, not as a preferred agent, even for patients with qualifying mutations, because more effective therapies (immune checkpoint inhibitors, BRAF-targeted therapy) should be tried first [MELSYS-3]. Patient selection by molecular screening is essential.

regimens.regimen_name: Binimetinib Monotherapy (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances for NRAS Mutant)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, NRAS Q61 mutation positive

trial_data.efficacy: ORR: 15%; median PFS: 2.8 months. Binimetinib demonstrated a modest improvement in PFS with no improvement in OS compared with single-agent dacarbazine in NRAS-mutant melanoma.

regimens.notes: Recommended for progression following immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy in NRAS mutation positive patients. The ORR and PFS are poor compared to BRAF inhibitors tested in other trials, so this is listed as useful in certain circumstances only [MELSYS-2, MELSYS-3].

regimens.regimen_name: Larotrectinib (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances for NTRK Fusion-Positive)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, NTRK1/2/3 gene fusion positive

trial_data.efficacy: High ORR demonstrated across tumor types for NTRK fusion-positive tumors.

regimens.notes: NTRK1/2/3 gene fusions occur uncommonly (<1%) across subtypes of melanoma. Larotrectinib has shown activity in NTRK fusion-positive tumors. Listed as useful in certain circumstances for this rare molecular subtype [MELSYS-2].

regimens.regimen_name: Entrectinib (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances for NTRK or ROS1 Fusion-Positive)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, NTRK1/2/3 or ROS1 gene fusion positive

biomarkers.marker: NTRK1/2/3 gene fusion or ROS1 gene fusion

trial_data.efficacy: Safety and antitumor activity demonstrated in NTRK/ROS1/ALK fusion-positive solid tumors.

regimens.notes: Entrectinib is a Pan-TRK, ROS1, and ALK inhibitor. Listed as useful in certain circumstances for patients with NTRK fusion-positive or ROS1 gene fusion-positive melanoma [MELSYS-2].

regimens.regimen_name: Crizotinib (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances for ROS1 Fusion-Positive)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, ROS1 gene fusion positive

trial_data.efficacy: Activity in ROS1 gene fusion-positive tumors based on case reports and limited clinical trial data.

regimens.notes: ROS1 gene fusions occur uncommonly (<1%) across subtypes of melanoma. Crizotinib is listed as useful in certain circumstances for ROS1 fusion-positive tumors [MELSYS-2].

regimens.regimen_name: Trametinib (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances for BRAF Non-V600 or Gene Fusion-Positive)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, BRAF non-V600 mutations or BRAF gene fusion

biomarkers.marker: BRAF non-V600 mutations (e.g., L597, K601) or BRAF gene fusion

trial_data.efficacy: BRAF gene fusion-positive tumors and certain BRAF non-V600 mutations (e.g., L597, K601) have shown responses to MEK inhibitors and non-specific RAF inhibitors.
reference_numbers: ME-C 7-8 (Hutchinson KE, et al. Clin Cancer Res 2013;19:6696-6702)
Dankner M, et al. Clin Cancer Res 2018;24:6483-6494

regimens.notes: BRAF non-V600 mutations include BRAF L597 and K601, which have shown response to MEK inhibitors and BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations. BRAF gene fusion-positive tumors have also shown responses to MEK inhibitors and non-specific RAF inhibitors [ME-C 2].

regimens.regimen_name: Cytotoxic Chemotherapy — Temozolomide (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: Similar efficacy to dacarbazine. Among cytotoxic agents, temozolomide and carboplatin/paclitaxel are preferred [MELSYS-5].

regimens.notes: Temozolomide and carboplatin/paclitaxel are preferred among cytotoxic agents for patients who are not candidates for further standard therapy [MELSYS-5]. Common cytotoxic agents used in metastatic melanoma include dacarbazine, temozolomide, and paclitaxel with or without carboplatin, demonstrating modest response rates less than 20% [MELSYS-5].

regimens.regimen_name: Cytotoxic Chemotherapy — Carboplatin/Paclitaxel (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: Modest response rates less than 20%. Among cytotoxic agents, carboplatin/paclitaxel is preferred along with temozolomide [MELSYS-5].

regimens.notes: Carboplatin/paclitaxel was added to the list of cytotoxic agent options in version 2.2026 [MELSYS 2 update]. Multiagent chemotherapy has shown a marginal improvement in response rate with no difference in OS when compared with single-agent dacarbazine [MELSYS-5].

regimens.regimen_name: Cytotoxic Chemotherapy — Dacarbazine (Second-Line/Subsequent — Useful in Certain Circumstances)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: Modest response rates less than 20%. No improvement in OS compared to other cytotoxic agents.

regimens.notes: Among cytotoxic agents, temozolomide and carboplatin/paclitaxel are preferred over dacarbazine [MELSYS-5]. Given the number of effective options available, cytotoxic therapy is not among the preferred options but may be considered for those who have failed or are ineligible for more effective options [MELSYS-3].

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic, previously treated

trial_data.efficacy: Multiagent chemotherapy has shown a marginal improvement in response rate with no difference in OS compared with single-agent dacarbazine.

regimens.notes: CVD is listed as useful in certain circumstances (category 2B) among the cytotoxic agent options [MELSYS 2]. Among cytotoxic agents, temozolomide and carboplatin/paclitaxel are preferred [MELSYS-5].

regimens.regimen_name: T-VEC (Intralesional) for Unresectable Stage III In-Transit Disease
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III (clinical satellite/in-transit), unresectable

drugs.name: Talimogene laherparepvec (T-VEC)
drugs.dose: Intralesional injection, up to 4 mL per lesion

trial_data.efficacy: DRR: 16.3% vs. 2.1% (P < .001). ORR: 26.4% vs. 5.7% (P < .001). CR in 11% vs. <1%. T-VEC associated with a response rate (lasting ≥6 months) of 16%.

regimens.notes: T-VEC is category 1 for patients with unresectable stage III in-transit disease based on improved durable and ORR compared to injection with GM-CSF alone [ME-8A]. For patients with low-volume in-transit disease, the high risk of toxicities associated with certain combination regimens may outweigh the benefit [ME-7A, ME-8A]. T-VEC has shown similar efficacy across clinically detected/macroscopic AJCC 8th Edition stage III disease [ME-8A]. Neoadjuvant T-VEC was associated with improved RFS at 2 years (29.5% vs. 16.5%) when followed by surgery versus surgery alone (n = 150) [ME-7A].

regimens.regimen_name: Interleukin-2 (IL-2) Intralesional (Stage III In-Transit — Useful in Certain Circumstances)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III (clinical satellite/in-transit), unresectable

trial_data.trial_name: Various phase II and observational studies
trial_data.efficacy: CR in injected lesions may be as high as 70%. 80% for dermal, 73% for subcutaneous lesions.

regimens.notes: Intralesional IL-2 is far less toxic than high-dose IV IL-2. Grade 1–2 adverse effects are common but manageable, and grade 3–4 toxicities are extremely rare [ME-8A]. Intralesional IL-2 is usually associated with an injection site inflammatory reaction with local swelling, erythema, pain, and sometimes necrosis [ME-8A]. Listed as useful in certain circumstances when T-VEC is not available [ME-8A].

regimens.regimen_name: Isolated Limb Infusion (ILI) with Melphalan-Based Regimen
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III (clinical satellite/in-transit), unresectable, extremity-only
regimens.setting: First-line (for unresectable in-transit disease confined to an extremity)

drugs.name: Melphalan
drugs.dose: Per institutional protocol (typically based on limb volume)

trial_data.efficacy: ORR: 73%. CR: 33% (range 26%–44% across studies). PR: 40%. SD: 14%.

regimens.notes: ILI is primarily used for patients with limb-only disease with progression on/contraindications to standard therapies. This procedure should only be done at centers with experience with ILI/ILP [ME-8A]. ILI is associated with lower rates of toxicity and morbidity compared with ILP [MS-41]. Addition of papaverine for cutaneous vasodilation has been shown to increase response rate but also the risk of regional toxicity [MS-41].

regimens.regimen_name: Isolated Limb Perfusion (ILP) with Melphalan-Based Regimen
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Stage III (clinical satellite/in-transit), unresectable, extremity-only
regimens.setting: First-line (for unresectable in-transit disease confined to an extremity)

trial_data.efficacy: Median ORR: 90% (range 64%–100%). Median CR rate: 58% (range 25%–89%). CR with single-agent melphalan: 47%; with melphalan/actinomycin D: 45%–65%; with melphalan/TNF-alfa: up to 70%.

regimens.notes: ILP is technically complex and invasive, making it challenging (or contraindicated) in elderly and frail patients, and difficult to reuse in the same patient in the event of recurrence or progression [MS-41]. A randomized trial comparing hyperthermic ILP with single-agent melphalan to combination melphalan and TNF-alfa did not show a significant difference in response rate [MS-41]. TNF-alfa is currently unavailable for use in the United States [MS-41].

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Unresectable stage III or Stage IV metastatic with brain metastases

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then 240 mg Q2W maintenance

trial_data.efficacy: CheckMate 204: Intracranial ORR 50%; intracranial CR 31%. CA209-170 cohort A (asymptomatic, no prior brain Tx): intracranial ORR 57%; intracranial PFS 13.8 months. Nivo1+ipi3 has demonstrated clinically meaningful intracranial activity.

regimens.notes: For systemic therapy in patients with asymptomatic brain metastasis not requiring corticosteroids, combination therapy with nivolumab/ipilimumab is preferred in comparison to anti-PD-1 monotherapy or BRAF/MEK inhibitors due to superior intracranial activity [ME-J, SYSCON-1]. Systemic corticosteroids may interfere with the efficacy of nivolumab/ipilimumab and should be avoided in patients being considered for combination nivolumab/ipilimumab [ME-J].

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — BRAF V600 mutant metastatic melanoma with brain metastases

trial_data.efficacy: Cohort A (asymptomatic, no prior systemic Tx, no prior local brain Tx): intracranial ORR 58%; median intracranial PFS 5.6 months; OS 10.8 months. Cohort D (symptomatic): intracranial ORR 59%; median intracranial PFS 5.5 months; OS 11.5 months.

regimens.notes: BRAF/MEK inhibitors result in a high intracranial response rate; however, PFS is shorter than reported data for extracranial disease. Patients treated with this approach are very likely to need subsequent brain-directed therapy and should be monitored closely [ME-J]. Dabrafenib/trametinib combination therapy may provide higher rates of response than single-agent BRAF inhibitor therapy [ME-J].

regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Melanoma brain metastases

trial_data.efficacy: 1-year local tumor control rates from 72% to 100% for patients with limited CNS disease, but lower rates for patients with multiple or large (>2 cm) tumors.
reference_numbers: Shaw 2000 (Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2000;47:291-298)

regimens.notes: SRS is the preferred radiation modality for melanoma brain metastases and can be delivered to multiple lesions depending on local experience and technology [ME-J]. Large lesions should be treated with fractionated SRS (3–5 fractions) to decrease the risk of radionecrosis [ME-J]. Adjuvant WBRT is not recommended after SRS/SRT for melanoma brain metastases [ME-J]. Dosing guidelines: lesions ≤20 mm receive up to 24 Gy; lesions 21–30 mm receive up to 18 Gy; lesions 31–40 mm receive up to 15 Gy [ME-H 3].

regimens.regimen_name: Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy (SBRT) for Extracranial Oligometastases
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Oligometastatic melanoma

trial_data.efficacy: Higher doses utilizing conformal techniques such as SBRT may offer more durable local control and freedom from regional or distant progression.

regimens.notes: SBRT may be considered for selected patients with oligometastasis [ME-H 4]. This potential benefit must be weighed against potential toxicities, and strict adherence to normal tissue constraints is recommended. Spine SBRT regimens include 16–24 Gy in 1 fraction, 20–24 Gy in 2 fractions, 24–27 Gy in 3 fractions, or 25–40 Gy in 5 fractions. SBRT for other body sites include 48–60 Gy in 3 fractions, 40–60 Gy in 4–5 fractions, or 16–24 Gy in 1 fraction [ME-H 4].

regimens.regimen_name: Pembrolizumab + Ipilimumab (Low-Dose) Combination Therapy (Brain Metastases — Alternative First-Line)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — BRAF V600 mutant metastatic melanoma, symptomatic brain metastases, untreated

trial_data.efficacy: Cohort D: intracranial ORR 59%; median intracranial PFS 5.5 months; OS 11.5 months.

regimens.notes: For select patients who are symptomatic with BRAF-mutated melanoma who have not been previously treated with a BRAF/MEK inhibitor, BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination therapy results in a high intracranial response rate. PFS is shorter than for extracranial disease. Patients treated with this approach are very likely to need subsequent brain-directed therapy and should be monitored closely [ME-J].

regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab (Adjuvant for Resected Stage IV — Preferred)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Resected stage IV metastatic disease (oligometastatic)

trial_data.efficacy: Nivolumab showed clinically significant improvement in RFS compared to high-dose ipilimumab. For stage IV resected patients, category 1.

regimens.notes: Category 1 for resected stage IV. The NCCN Panel agrees that it is appropriate to extend the indication for adjuvant anti-PD-1 therapy to patients with clinical or macroscopic satellite/in-transit disease and who are at significant risk of recurrence [ADJSYS-1].

regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab (Adjuvant for Resected Stage IV with Prior Anti-PD-1 — Category 1)
regimens.site: Melanoma: Cutaneous — Resected stage IV metastatic disease with prior anti-PD-1 exposure

drugs.name: Ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then every 12 weeks for up to 3 years

trial_data.efficacy: Ipilimumab is included as an adjuvant treatment option for patients with resected stage IV disease who have prior exposure to anti-PD-1 agents based on extrapolation of data demonstrating efficacy as adjuvant treatment for resected stage III disease.

regimens.notes: Category 1 for resected nodal recurrence with prior anti-PD-1. Ipilimumab is no longer a recommended first-line adjuvant option for stage III disease at presentation but remains useful for patients who progress during anti-PD-1 therapy with resectable disease [ADJSYS-1]. The recommended dose is 3 mg/kg.

regimens.site: Metastatic uveal melanoma, HLA-A*02:01-positive
regimens.notes: Tebentafusp-tebn is a bispecific gp100-HLA-A*02:01-directed T-cell receptor and CD3 T-cell engager. Treatment resulted in longer overall survival compared to investigator's choice of pembrolizumab, ipilimumab, or dacarbazine. Response rates are low, so symptomatic patients may be better palliated by liver-directed treatment first or may respond better to ipilimumab/nivolumab. [526, 537]

regimens.site: Metastatic uveal melanoma

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II trials (Pelster et al, GEM-1402)

regimens.notes: Nivolumab/ipilimumab has shown modest improvement in OS over historical benchmarks of chemotherapy. The dose used is nivolumab 1 mg/kg and ipilimumab 3 mg/kg. It is unknown whether other dosing regimens are equally effective. [534, 535]

regimens.site: Metastatic uveal melanoma

drugs.name: Ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: every 3 weeks for 4 cycles, then every 12 weeks

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II studies (Piulats et al, Zimmer et al)

regimens.notes: Ipilimumab monotherapy has shown limited clinical activity in metastatic uveal melanoma. Response rates are low. [527, 528]

regimens.site: Metastatic uveal melanoma
regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is an anti-PD-1 antibody. It has shown low response rates in uveal melanoma. [530, 531]

regimens.site: Metastatic uveal melanoma
regimens.notes: Nivolumab monotherapy is an option for patients with disease progression on ipilimumab. [532, 533]

regimens.site: Metastatic uveal melanoma
regimens.notes: Dacarbazine is a cytotoxic chemotherapy with low response rates in uveal melanoma. [522, 524]

regimens.site: Metastatic uveal melanoma
regimens.notes: Temozolomide is an oral alkylating agent with low response rates. [547]

regimens.site: Metastatic uveal melanoma
regimens.notes: Paclitaxel is a taxane chemotherapy with low response rates. [539]

regimens.site: Metastatic uveal melanoma
regimens.notes: Albumin-bound paclitaxel is a nanoparticle formulation of paclitaxel. [540]

regimens.site: Metastatic uveal melanoma
regimens.notes: Carboplatin/paclitaxel is a combination chemotherapy regimen. [548]

regimens.site: Metastatic uveal melanoma
regimens.notes: Trametinib is a MEK inhibitor. It is included based on positive data for selumetinib and the general lack of systemic therapy options. [555, 563]

regimens.site: Metastatic uveal melanoma, hepatic-dominant

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II and phase III trials

regimens.notes: Melphalan percutaneous hepatic perfusion is a liver-directed therapy for patients with hepatic-dominant metastatic uveal melanoma with <50% liver involvement. It requires overnight stay in ICU for hemodynamic monitoring and should be performed only at experienced centers. [472, 473]
